title: METR survey: technical workers self-report 1.4–2x value uplift from AI in March 2026, with strong caveats
summary: METR published results from a 349-respondent survey of software engineers, researchers, academics, and founders measuring self-reported AI productivity impact. Median reported value-of-work multiplier was 1.4–2x for March 2026, with a higher 3x median for speed-of-task; respondents retrospectively estimated 1.3x in March 2025 and forecast 2.5x by March 2027. METR explicitly cautions that surveys overestimate productivity: their own 2025 controlled trial found experienced OSS developers were 19% slower with AI tools while feeling 20% faster — a ~40-percentage-point delusion gap. Subgroup variation is large: heavy Claude Code users report the highest gains, while METR's own employees report notably lower gains. The headline number is therefore best read as a perception trend, not a measured productivity outcome.
whyItMatters: Many vendor ROI claims and exec dashboards are still built on self-reported numbers; this study quantifies how unreliable that signal is, and reinforces that leaders should not size investments or headcount decisions on perception alone.

The gap between speed (3x) and value (1.4–2x) suggests AI mostly substitutes for the easy parts of work — leaders should look for value capture upstream of coding (scope, review, integration) rather than only at keystrokes.
Subgroup variation by tool and role means org-wide averages hide where AI is actually working; finer measurement at the team level is needed before standardizing tooling.
Forecast growth to 2.5x by 2027 will continue to be used as a planning input despite the caveats — engineering finance teams should pressure-test it against telemetry.
recommendedActions: Pair self-report data with controlled or quasi-experimental measurement (matched-pair PRs, randomized task assignment) before making productivity claims to the board.
Track speed and value as separate metrics in internal dashboards; do not treat 'felt faster' as evidence of business throughput.
Run a small replication of METR's design on your own engineers to calibrate the perception gap in your environment.
risksAndCaveats: Sample is small (n=349), recruited through METR's network, and ~70% incentivized — selection bias is substantial.
Retrospective estimates of March 2025 productivity are subject to recall and post-hoc rationalization.
Findings are about perception and forecasts, not measured throughput, defect rates, or revenue impact.
